assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Caesar's English II, notebook

➜ INTRO
This text is best enjoyed by reading the assigned pages aloud and working through the various activities with your teacher.
You will want to take advantage of QR codes on specific pages, as the author brings things to life!

➜ READ ALOUD
p.5-8

➜ LISTEN
Listen to the author introduce the text at the QR code at the bottom of p.9.

➜ READ ALOUD & DISCUSS
p.9-18

• Copy the Latin Stem List with the meaning in your notebook.
• Can you think of other modern examples?
• Think about the power of a stem when looking at p.14.
• Pick a word or two from the list on p.15. Orally compose or write a sentence using it.
• Check your answers for lesson activities.
∞ PDF Link: Lesson Answer Key

• EXTRA HELPINGS
If interested, search "[stem] root word examples" online for more. For example: "Com root word examples."

⍞ Materials: Caesar's English II, notebook

➜ READ ALOUD, COMPLETE, & DISCUSS
p.19-23

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
Listen and follow along as the author reads his poem at the QR code at the bottom of p.24.

• Underline any of the vocabulary words from the chapter.
• Do you see any poetic devices? Any alliteration. assonance, end rhyme? Others?

➜ READ ALOUD & DISCUSS
p.25-27

➜ PRACTICE
Orally work through the Lesson 1 Quiz.
∞ PDF Link: Quiz
∞ PDF Link: Quiz Answer Key

⍞ Materials: Caesar's English II, notebook

➜ READ ALOUD, COMPLETE, & DISCUSS
p.28-35

• Copy down the classic words and their definitions into your notebook.

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
Listen and follow along as the author reads his story at the QR code at the bottom of p.36.

• After listening to the story read, work out its meaning together. Look back at the chapters for the meaning of words you cannot remember.
• Underline the vocabulary words and identify their parts of speech.

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
Listen and follow along as the author reads his poem at the QR code at the bottom of p.37.

• Underline any of the vocabulary words from the chapter.
• Do you see any poetic devices? What is the rhyme scheme?

➜ READ ALOUD & DISCUSS

• As other composition lessons are assigned for the week, skip the assignment on p.67. If interested, feel free to complete the assignment on your own at another time.

∞ PDF Link: Quiz

⍞ Materials: Caesar's English II, notebook

p.72-76

• Copy the list of Nonfiction Words and their stems and meanings into your notebook.

➜ LISTEN, COMPLETE, & DISCUSS
Listen and follow along as the author reads the story at the QR code at the bottom of p.77.

• Try your hand at writing your own Sesquipedalian Story. Read it aloud.
• Skip the essay writing on p.77. If interested, feel free to complete the assignment on your own at another time.

➜ READ ALOUD, COMPLETE, & DISCUSS
p.78-81

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
Listen and follow along as the author reads the sentences and the poem at the QR code at the bottom of p.80, 82.

★ STUDENT/TEACHER NOTE
Since we have composition lessons built into the curriculum using all your reading as context for writing, we won't utilize the essay writing assignments mentioned in this book. But do be sure to read the essay instruction, as it will be a good reminder of what you learned in Essay Voyage last year and helpful for your writing assignments this year and going forward.

➜ LISTEN, COMPLETE, & DISCUSS

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Grammar: Grade 10 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Caesar's English II: Write a sesquipedalian story using seven of the following words: placate, vivacious, surfeit, benevolent, profuse, sublunar, alacrity, declaim, and revitalize. (You may change their part of speech if needed. For example: revitalize to revitalization.)

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Grammar: Grade 10 — TERM 2 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Caesar's English II: Write a sesquipedalian story using 10 of the following words: verdure, tumult, equivocal, germane, nonplussed, infusion, ignominy, impending, gratuitous, migratory, tacit, sanguine, mortify, elucidate, importune, pensive, magnanimous. (You may change their part of speech if needed. For example: revitalize to revitalization.)
